core_concept.explanation: sp³ hybridization occurs when one $s$ orbital mixes with three $p$ orbitals to form four equivalent $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals. These orbitals arrange themselves in a tetrahedral geometry with $109.5^\circ$ bond angles to minimize electron repulsion. This hybridization state is characteristic of saturated carbon atoms (alkanes) forming only single ($\sigma$) bonds.

image.alt: Diagram showing one s and three p orbitals mixing to form four sp³ orbitals
image.caption: Mixing one s and three p orbitals yields four equivalent sp³ hybrids

sections.introduction: sp³ hybridization is observed in atoms with a steric number of 4, typically involving four single bonds or a combination of bonds and lone pairs. This hybridization state provides 25% s-character, resulting in the longest and weakest bonds among the common hybridization states (sp³ > sp² > sp), but allows for free rotation around sigma bonds.

introduction_image.alt: Tetrahedral geometry showing 109.5 degree bond angles
introduction_image.caption: sp³ hybrid orbitals orient tetrahedrally at 109.5 degrees

key_concept.definition: sp³ hybrid orbitals are formed by the linear combination of one s and three p atomic orbitals, resulting in four tetrahedrally arranged hybrids.
key_concept.context: Fundamental to alkane chemistry, saturated carbons, and atoms with lone pairs like nitrogen in ammonia or oxygen in water.

image.alt: Shape of sp³ hybrid orbitals showing tetrahedral arrangement
image.caption: Four sp³ orbitals point to the corners of a tetrahedron

classifications.value: Steric Number = 4 (4 sigma bonds OR 3 bonds + 1 lone pair OR 2 bonds + 2 lone pairs)
classifications.behavior: Forms four sigma bonds or uses hybrids for lone pairs; allows free rotation around single bonds

examples.explanation: Carbon forms 4 sigma bonds with hydrogen. All bond angles are 109.5°.

image.alt: Structure of methane showing tetrahedral geometry
image.caption: Methane is the simplest alkane with sp³ carbon

examples.explanation: Nitrogen is sp³ hybridized with 3 bonds and 1 lone pair. Geometry is trigonal pyramidal.
examples.value: Bond angle compressed to 107° due to lone pair repulsion

examples.explanation: Oxygen is sp³ hybridized with 2 bonds and 2 lone pairs. Geometry is bent.

image.alt: Water molecule showing sp³ hybridization and bent shape
image.caption: Lone pairs occupy sp³ orbitals, compressing bond angle to 104.5°

principles.application: No unhybridized $p$ orbitals remain, meaning sp³ carbons cannot form $\pi$ bonds (no double/triple bonds).
principles.analogy: Like having four hands (sp³ hybrids) reaching out to the corners of a pyramid to hold objects.

image.alt: Diagram showing orbital count conservation in sp³ hybridization
image.caption: Total orbitals before and after hybridization remain constant (4)

principles.statement: sp³ hybrids have 25% s-character, the lowest among sp³, sp², and sp.
principles.application: Lower s-character means electrons are held less tightly to the nucleus, resulting in longer, weaker bonds and lower acidity.
principles.example: Ethane C-H bond length (110 pm) > Ethene C-H (108 pm) > Ethyne C-H (106 pm).

image.alt: Comparison of bond lengths for sp³, sp², and sp hybridized carbons
image.caption: Bond length increases as s-character decreases

principles.statement: Single bonds formed by sp³ hybrids allow free rotation around the bond axis.
principles.application: Unlike double bonds, single bonds do not lock geometry, enabling conformational isomerism (e.g., staggered vs. eclipsed).
principles.example: Ethane ($CH_3-CH_3$) can rotate around the C-C bond without breaking it.

image.alt: Rotation around single bond in ethane
image.caption: Free rotation around sigma bonds enables different conformations

components.definition: The sp³ hybrid wavefunction combines s and three p orbitals: $\psi_{sp^3} = \frac{1}{2}(\phi_s + \phi_{p_x} + \phi_{p_y} + \phi_{p_z})$
components.interpretation: The 25% s-character provides the least electron density near the nucleus among common hybrids, influencing reactivity and bond strength.

image.alt: Mathematical representation of sp³ hybrid wavefunction
image.caption: Equal contribution from one s and three p orbitals defines sp³ hybrids

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Bonding in Ethane (C₂H₆)
worked_examples.problem: Determine the hybridization of each carbon in ethane, describe the orbital overlap involved in the C-C single bond, and explain the molecular geometry.

step_by_step.explanation: Each carbon is bonded to 3 H atoms and 1 C atom. No lone pairs. Steric Number = 4.
Steric number 4 requires 4 hybrid orbitals. Mix one s and three p.
All three p orbitals are used in hybridization. None remain.
C-C sigma bond: $sp^3-sp^3$ overlap. C-H sigma bonds: $sp^3-s$ overlap. All bonds are sigma.

worked_examples.final_answer: Each carbon is sp³ hybridized. Geometry around each carbon is tetrahedral (109.5°). The C-C bond is a sigma bond allowing free rotation.
worked_examples.concept_applied: sp³ hybridization enables tetrahedral geometry and single bond formation with free rotation.

image.alt: Orbital overlap diagram for ethane single bond
image.caption: Sigma framework formed entirely by sp³ hybrid overlap

process_flow.description: Look for atoms with only single bonds (no double or triple bonds) and no resonance requiring p orbitals.
process_flow.mechanism: Single bonds require only sigma overlap, compatible with full hybridization.
process_flow.result: Candidate atoms identified for sp³ hybridization

image.alt: Examples of molecules with sp³ centers
image.caption: Identify atoms involved in single bonds only

process_flow.description: Confirm the atom has exactly 4 electron domains (bonded atoms + lone pairs).

image.alt: Steric number calculation for sp³ atom
image.caption: Steric number of 4 confirms sp³ hybridization

process_flow.description: Arrange the four sp³ orbitals tetrahedrally. Adjust for lone pairs if present.
process_flow.mechanism: VSEPR theory minimizes repulsion between 4 electron domains
process_flow.result: Tetrahedral (0 LP), Trigonal Pyramidal (1 LP), or Bent (2 LP)

image.alt: Geometric variations of sp³ hybridization
image.caption: Lone pairs occupy sp³ orbitals but alter molecular shape

components.function: Form four sigma bonds in a tetrahedral arrangement
components.key_feature: 109.5° bond angles optimize orbital separation in 3D space

image.alt: Detailed view of sp³ hybrid orbital arrangement
image.caption: Four sp³ orbitals point to corners of a tetrahedron

components.function: Not available; all p orbitals consumed in hybridization
components.key_feature: Absence of unhybridized p orbitals prevents pi bonding

image.alt: Diagram showing no remaining p orbitals
image.caption: No unhybridized p orbitals remain for pi bonding

function.operation: sp³ hybrids form the flexible sigma framework of saturated molecules.
function.energy: sp³ C-H bonds are the weakest among hybridization states; C-C single bonds allow conformational flexibility.
function.mechanism: Tetrahedral geometry minimizes steric strain; sigma bonds allow rotation without breaking overlap.

image.alt: Function diagram showing sigma framework and rotation
image.caption: Sigma bonds provide structure and flexibility via rotation

cycle.operation: Head-on overlap of sp³ hybrids with partner atoms (C, H, O, N)

image.alt: Sigma bond formation in sp³ system
image.caption: sp³ hybrids form the tetrahedral sigma backbone

cycle.operation: Rotation around sigma bond axis without breaking overlap
cycle.purpose: Allows molecule to adopt lowest energy conformations (e.g., staggered)

image.alt: Rotation around sp³-sp³ sigma bond
image.caption: Free rotation enables conformational isomerism

categories.type: Effect of Lone Pairs on sp³ Geometry
categories.description: While electron geometry remains tetrahedral, molecular geometry changes when lone pairs occupy sp³ orbitals.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Lone pairs exert greater repulsion than bonding pairs (LP-BP > BP-BP), compressing bond angles below 109.5°.

examples.explanation: 1 lone pair compresses H-N-H angle from 109.5° to 107°. Shape: Trigonal Pyramidal.

image.alt: Geometry of ammonia
image.caption: Lone pair reduces bond angle slightly

examples.explanation: 2 lone pairs compress H-O-H angle from 109.5° to 104.5°. Shape: Bent.

image.alt: Geometry of water
image.caption: Two lone pairs reduce bond angle further

image.alt: Comparison of bond angles with lone pairs
image.caption: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs

summary: sp³ hybridization involves mixing one $s$ and three $p$ orbitals to create four tetrahedral $sp^3$ hybrid orbitals oriented $109.5^\circ$ apart. This leaves no unhybridized $p$ orbitals, meaning sp³ centers form only $\sigma$ bonds (single bonds) and cannot participate in $\pi$ bonding. sp³-hybridized carbons exhibit 25% s-character, producing the longest and weakest bonds among common hybridization states, but enabling free rotation around single bonds. This hybridization is fundamental to saturated organic chemistry (alkanes) and explains the geometry of molecules with lone pairs (e.g., $NH_3$, $H_2O$) where electron geometry remains tetrahedral but molecular geometry varies.
